unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: This is the last unit of Year 2, and it gathers up everything you have learned. You revise thirty words from Units 1 to 9, from name and neighbour to butterfly, hut and underground, and you work through six grammar workshops that bring back pronouns, questions, commands, comparisons, There is and There are, and the past, present and future. Then you plan, draft, revise and publish an illustrated six-page booklet called My English World, and you present it at the class showcase just as Amal does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Read Amal's English Year, the project brief and the Showcase Day dialogue, then answer the twelve comprehension questions.
Revise the thirty review words in the nine unit groups and build your own word bank.
Complete the six cumulative grammar workshops, one for each pattern of the year.
Plan, draft, revise, edit and publish the six-page My English World booklet.
Rehearse in the six live sessions and deliver your two-minute showcase presentation.
Finish with the cumulative quiz, the portfolio assignment and your final reflection.

readings.title: Amal's English Year
readings.theme: Looking back on a year of learning
readings.setting: A Grade 2 classroom on the last two weeks of Year 2
readings.passageScript:
Amal opened her English folder on the last Monday of Year 2. At the front she found her welcome card from Unit 1. Her name was written in careful blue letters. Behind it lay a poem about her neighbour Theo. Next to the poem was a drawing of Leila the firefighter.

Amal turned another page. She found her shadow chart from the big sky unit. She found a measuring table and a bug fact file. She found her garden promise as well. Last of all she found her city map from Unit 9.

Teacher Yasmin smiled at the class. “This term you will build one last project,” she said. “It is called My English World.” Every child had to choose six pages from six different units.

Amal read her old work again. She fixed one sentence on her helpers page. She had written “The firefighters is working.” Now she wrote “The firefighters are working.” She added two describing words to her sky page. Unit 4 had taught her about adjectives.

Then Amal made her new booklet. Page one told the reader her name and her class. Page two showed a helpful neighbour. Page three showed the parts of the body. Page four showed the sun and a long shadow. Page five showed her house. Page six showed the library in the city.

On showcase day Amal stood beside her table. Her hands felt cold, so she breathed slowly. “Welcome to my English world,” she said. Nora and Leo listened, and Grandma Hana clapped.

At the end Amal wrote one last reflection. “I can read, write and speak more clearly now,” she wrote. “Next year I want to read a long chapter book.” She closed her folder and felt proud.

readings.title: The My English World Project Brief
readings.theme: Planning, making and presenting a booklet
readings.setting: The classroom noticeboard and the online capstone page
readings.passageScript:
My English World is your last project of Year 2. It has four parts, and you work through them in order.

Part 1: Choose your pages. Pick six pages from at least four different units. Look for work that shows reading, writing, speaking and new words.

Part 2: Make the booklet. Write two full sentences on every page. Add one picture and one label to each page.

Part 3: Present your work. Speak for about two minutes. Point at your pictures and answer one question from a listener.

Part 4: Reflect. Write three sentences about what you can do now. Then write one goal for Year 3.

Read every page aloud before you hand it in. Your ears will find the missing full stops. Ask a partner for one kind idea and one careful question. Fix what you can, and keep the pages that are already strong.

readings.title: Showcase Day
readings.setting: The Grade 2 English showcase in the school hall
readings.passageScript:
Teacher Yasmin: Welcome, everybody. Amal will present first.
Amal: Hello. My name is Amal. My booklet is called My English World.
Teacher Yasmin: Which page are you most proud of?
Amal: Page four. It shows the sun and my long shadow. Unit 4 taught me why shadows grow longer in the evening.
Nora: I like your bug page too. The butterfly has beautiful wings.
Amal: Thank you. I labelled the wings and the antennae.
Leo: How did you make the plant page better?
Amal: I read it aloud to Adam. He said it was too short. I added two facts about planting.
Teacher Yasmin: That is careful work. What is your goal for next year?
Amal: I want to read a long chapter book by myself.
Teacher Yasmin: Thank you, Amal. Nora, you may present now.

grammar.title: Pronouns and Likes (Units 1 and 9)
grammar.explanation: In Unit 1 you learned to use he, she and they so that you do not repeat a name in every sentence. Use like with I, you, we and they, and use likes with he, she or one person's name.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Amal presents her booklet. She points at page four.
Leo and Sami wait their turn. They clap for Amal.
Amal likes her bug page. Her friends like the city map.
Grandma Hana likes the shadow drawing.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “Amal like her booklet.” One person needs likes, so the sentence becomes “Amal likes her booklet.”
grammar.memoryTip: One person takes the s: he likes, she likes. Two or more people drop it: we like, they like.
grammar.practice:
Rewrite each pair so the second sentence starts with he, she or they.
1. Nora finished her booklet. Nora is proud of page two.
2. Leo and Daniel made a city map. Leo and Daniel used a ruler.
3. Teacher Yasmin read every reflection. Teacher Yasmin wrote a kind note.
4. Amal ______ (like / likes) the library page best of all.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

grammar.title: Asking Clear Questions with Who, What, Where and How (Units 2 and 6)
grammar.explanation: In Unit 2 you asked who and what about the helpers on your street. In Unit 6 you asked where and how about bugs. A question always finishes with a question mark.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Who cleans the high windows? Karim cleans them.
What does a reporter do? She finds out the news.
Where does the cricket hide? It hides in the long grass.
How did you make your booklet better? I read it aloud to Adam.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “Who cleans the windows.” The sentence asks something, so it needs a question mark: “Who cleans the windows?”
grammar.memoryTip: Say the sentence aloud. If your voice lifts at the end, the sentence needs a question mark.
grammar.practice:
Write the question word that fits, then add the question mark.
1. ______ keeps the road safe outside our school
2. ______ time does the showcase begin
3. ______ does the underground train run
4. ______ did you fix your plant page
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

grammar.title: Giving Commands for Showcase Day (Unit 3)
grammar.explanation: In Unit 3 you gave movement commands such as Hop and Clap. A command starts with the plain verb and tells somebody what to do. Commands are useful when you teach a visitor how to read your booklet.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Look at page one first.
Turn the page slowly.
Point at the butterfly wings.
Read the label aloud, please.
Ask me one question at the end.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “You looking at page one.” A command needs the plain verb at the front, so it becomes “Look at page one.”
grammar.memoryTip: A command begins with the doing word and ends with a full stop or an exclamation mark.
grammar.practice:
Turn each sentence into a command for a showcase visitor.
1. You should hold the booklet carefully.
2. You should read the labels out loud.
3. You should ask me about page four.
4. Write two more commands of your own for your showcase table.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

grammar.title: Describing and Comparing with Adjectives and -er Than (Units 4 and 5)
grammar.explanation: Unit 4 taught you describing words such as bright, dark and cloudy. Unit 5 taught you to compare two things with -er than. Together they make every booklet page clearer.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One thing: The sky is bright. My shadow is long. The hut is small.
Two things: My shadow is longer than Leo's shadow.
The metre stick is taller than the ruler.
The apartment block is higher than the little hut.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “My shadow is more long than yours.” Short describing words take -er, so it becomes “My shadow is longer than yours.”
grammar.memoryTip: Add -er to a short describing word, then say than before the second thing you are comparing.
grammar.practice:
Finish each sentence with a describing word or with an -er than comparison.
1. The morning light in our classroom is ______.
2. The butterfly is ______ than the ant.
3. My city map page is ______ than my first draft.
4. Write one sentence describing your favourite booklet page.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

grammar.title: There Is, There Are and Place Words (Units 6 and 8)
grammar.explanation: Unit 6 taught you position words such as on, under, in and between. Unit 8 taught you There is for one thing and There are for more than one. Together they help you describe your showcase table.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
There is one booklet on the table.
There are three pictures beside the booklet.
The butterfly card is under the city map.
The pencil pot sits between the folder and the lamp.
There is one ant in the garden picture.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “There is three pictures.” Three pictures are more than one, so the sentence becomes “There are three pictures.”
grammar.memoryTip: Count first. One thing takes There is, and two or more things take There are.
grammar.practice:
Write There is or There are, then add a place word.
1. ______ four labels ______ page two.
2. ______ one shadow ______ the tall tree.
3. ______ two chairs ______ the showcase table.
4. Describe your own table in two sentences, using There is once and There are once.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

grammar.title: Past, Present and Future with -ed, -ing and Will (Units 4, 7, 8 and 9)
grammar.explanation: Unit 4 showed you past verbs ending in -ed. Units 3 and 7 showed you actions happening now with -ing. Units 8 and 9 showed you will for the future. Your reflection page needs all three.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Past: Last term I planted a tree with Adam.
Now: Today I am reading my booklet to the class.
Future: Next year I will read a long chapter book.
Past: We watered the seeds every morning.
Now: Nora is watering the class plants.
Future: Nora will present after me.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “Yesterday I will plant a tree.” Yesterday is in the past, so the sentence becomes “Yesterday I planted a tree.”
grammar.memoryTip: Look for the time word first. Yesterday takes -ed, today takes am or is with -ing, and next year takes will.
grammar.practice:
Write each sentence with the right verb form.
1. Last week we (pick up) ______ litter near the school gate.
2. Right now Leo (read) ______ his reflection aloud.
3. Next year I (write) ______ longer stories.
4. Write one past, one present and one future sentence about your own English year.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
grammar.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Introduce My English World
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Introduce your project in about thirty seconds. Say three things in this order.
Your name: “My name is ______.”
Your title: “My booklet is called My English World.”
Your units: “I chose pages from Unit ______, Unit ______, Unit ______ and Unit ______.”
Record it once, listen back, then record it again more slowly.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, play your thirty-second introduction to the tutor and ask whether your four unit numbers were clear.
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
speaking.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Vocabulary Spotlight
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose ten words from the review dictionary, one from each unit group plus one extra. For every word say the word, then say what it means, then use it in a sentence about your booklet.
Model: “Shadow. A shadow is a dark shape made when something blocks the light. My shadow is on page four.”
Record all ten words in one take.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to say one review word while your helper listens, then give the meaning and a sentence before the tutor tells you the answer.

speaking.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Read Aloud with Expression
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Read the dialogue Showcase Day with a partner. One of you reads Amal and Teacher Yasmin; the other reads Nora and Leo. Pause at every full stop and lift your voice at every question mark. Then swap parts and read it again.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read one part of the showcase dialogue to the tutor, with an adult present, and ask it to tell you where to pause next time.
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
speaking.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Explain My Best Page

“The hardest part was ______, and I fixed it by ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Answer a Visitor's Question
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:

“How did you make this page better?”
“What was hard about this project?”

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — The Final Capstone Presentation

writing.title: Writing 1 — Plan the Six Pages
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw a grid of six boxes. In each box write the unit number, the page topic, one picture idea and two sentence ideas. Your six pages must come from at least four different units.
writing.modelText: Page 1: Unit 1, About Me. Page 2: Unit 2, A Helpful Neighbour. Page 3: Unit 3, My Healthy Body. Page 4: Unit 4, The Sun and My Shadow. Page 5: Unit 8, My Home. Page 6: Unit 9, A Place in My City.
writing.expectedLength: Six planning boxes, each with four short notes
writing.successCriteria: I named a unit for every page; I wrote one picture idea and two sentence ideas per page; I used at least four different units.
writing.support: Use the review dictionary and your Grade 2 folder to remind you what each unit taught.
writing.extension: Add a seventh box for a page you would make if you had more time.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

writing.title: Writing 2 — Build the Review Word Bank
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose thirty review words, then sort them under your six page headings. Beside each word write the meaning in your own words. Highlight the five words you find hardest to spell.
writing.modelText: Page 4 words: sun, light, shadow. Shadow means a dark shape made when something blocks the light.
writing.sentenceStarter: The words I need for this page are
writing.expectedLength: Thirty words sorted under six headings
writing.successCriteria: Every word is spelled correctly; every meaning is written in my own words; my five hardest words are highlighted.
writing.support: Copy the meanings from the review dictionary first, then close it and write them again from memory.
writing.extension: Add two more words of your own for any page, and explain why you chose them.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

writing.title: Writing 3 — Draft the Booklet
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write the first draft of all six pages. Put two full sentences on each page and leave a box for the picture. Write on every other line so you have room to change things later.
writing.modelText: Page 2: Theo is our neighbour, and he lives in the blue house. He waves to us from his gate every morning.
writing.sentenceStarter: On this page you can see
writing.expectedLength: Twelve sentences in total, two on each of six pages
writing.successCriteria: Every page has two full sentences; every sentence has a capital letter and a full stop; the words match the page topic.
writing.support: Read your plan from Writing 1 aloud before you start each page.
writing.extension: Join two of your sentences with and, but or because.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

writing.title: Writing 4 — Revise with a Partner
writing.promptAndInstructions: Swap drafts with your partner. Write one thing you liked and one careful question on their feedback slip. Then take your own draft back and make two changes because of what your partner said. Circle the two changes in colour.
writing.modelText: I liked your butterfly page because the words are exact. My question is: which insect part is the antenna?
writing.expectedLength: One feedback slip given, two circled changes made
writing.successCriteria: My feedback names something real; my question is kind; I changed two things and I can say why.
writing.support: Use the sentence frames: “I liked ______ because ______.” and “My question is ______?”
writing.extension: Make a third change that nobody suggested, and explain your reason underneath.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

writing.title: Writing 5 — Edit and Publish
writing.promptAndInstructions: Read every page aloud and check four things: capital letters, full stops and question marks, spelling of your review words, and the verb form. Then copy the pages out neatly, add your pictures and write one label on each page.
writing.modelText: Checked page three: capital letters correct, full stops correct, finger spelled correctly, and the verb is are, not is.
writing.sentenceStarter: When I read page ______ aloud, I noticed
writing.expectedLength: Six published pages with pictures and labels
writing.successCriteria: I read every page aloud; I fixed the errors I heard; every page has one picture and one label.
writing.support: Tick each of the four checks on the editing card before you move to the next page.
writing.extension: Design a front cover with the title, your name and one small drawing.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

writing.title: Writing 6 — Write the Final Reflection
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five sentences about your English year. Use one past sentence, one present sentence and one future sentence. Name one thing that was hard in Year 2 and say how you got better at it.
writing.modelText: In Unit 2 I wrote three short sentences. Now I write two full pages. Next year I will read a long chapter book by myself.
writing.sentenceStarter: At the start of Year 2 I could
writing.expectedLength: Five sentences, including past, present and future
writing.successCriteria: I used -ed for the past, -ing for now and will for the future; I named one hard thing; I set one clear goal.
writing.support: Look back at your Unit 1 work, then at your booklet, and say the difference out loud before you write.
writing.extension: Add a sentence that thanks somebody who helped you this year.
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
writing.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

assignments.title: My English World capstone portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit four things: the published six-page booklet, three chosen portfolio pieces with a reason for each, a recording of your two-minute presentation, and your final reflection with one goal for Year 3.
Marking guidance for the teacher. Award the 64 marks as follows.
Booklet, 24 marks: 4 marks for each of the six pages. Give 2 marks for two accurate sentences, 1 mark for a matching picture and 1 mark for a correct label.
Portfolio choice, 12 marks: 4 marks for each piece, awarded for naming the unit, naming the skill and giving a real reason.
Presentation, 16 marks: use the Speaking rubric criteria of clarity, vocabulary, sentence accuracy and interaction, 4 marks each.
Reflection, 12 marks: 4 marks for a past sentence with -ed, 4 marks for a present sentence with -ing, and 4 marks for a future goal with will.
Do not award marks for drawing skill or for the materials a family can afford. Assess the English only, and accept scribed or recorded work where a learner needs it.
assignments.submissionType: Illustrated booklet + portfolio evidence + audio or video presentation + written reflection
assignments.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
assignments.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

liveSessions.afterSession: Draft at least four of your six pages before the next session.

liveSessions.title: Peer feedback conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your complete first draft, written on every other line.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min model a kind conference; 20 min Peer Feedback Conference (Activity 4); 15 min make two circled changes (Writing 4); 5 min share one change you made
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish your revision and start copying out your neatest pages.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
liveSessions.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

liveSessions.title: Editing, publishing and rehearsal
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your revised draft, your pictures and your glue.
liveSessions.agenda: 10 min the four editing checks (Writing 5); 15 min publish pages and add labels; 15 min Two-Minute Rehearsal Timer (Activity 6); 5 min timing tips
liveSessions.afterSession: Rehearse your presentation once at home to an adult or an older sibling.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
liveSessions.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

liveSessions.title: The My English World showcase
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your published booklet, your three portfolio pieces and your name card.
liveSessions.agenda: 10 min Showcase Table Setup (Activity 5); 20 min presentations in two circles (Speaking 6); 10 min visitors ask one question each; 5 min class applause and reflection prompt
liveSessions.afterSession: Write your final reflection and name one English goal for Year 3.

teacherNotes.note:
This unit revises Year 2. Use the map below when you confer with a learner, so the child can name where each idea came from.
Vocabulary groups: group 1 revises Unit 1 (name, partner, like, spell); group 2 revises Unit 2 (neighbour, window cleaner, police officer, reporter); group 3 revises Unit 3 (head, arm, hand, finger); group 4 revises Unit 4 (shadow, light, sun); group 5 revises Unit 5 (ten, twenty, thirty); group 6 revises Unit 6 (butterfly, cricket, ant); group 7 revises Unit 7 (planting, watering, picking up); group 8 revises Unit 8 (house, apartment, hut); group 9 revises Unit 9 (library, shopping center, underground).
Grammar workshops: Grammar 1 revises Units 1 and 9 (pronouns, like and likes); Grammar 2 revises Units 2 and 6 (question words and question marks); Grammar 3 revises Unit 3 (commands); Grammar 4 revises Units 4 and 5 (adjectives and -er than); Grammar 5 revises Units 6 and 8 (There is, There are and place words); Grammar 6 revises Units 4, 7, 8 and 9 (past -ed, present -ing and future will).
Cast: Amal is the same Grade 2 girl the class has followed since Unit 1. Teacher Yasmin, Nora, Leo, Sami, Maya, Daniel and Theo keep the roles they held all year, as do Adam and Idris (Amal's brothers), Mina (her sister), Grandma Hana, Omar the shopkeeper, Karim the window cleaner, Rami the police officer and Leila the firefighter. Never swap a role between names.
Treat the capstone as supported independent performance. Confer briefly with each learner, keep the child's own language on the page, and give one strength and one manageable next step.
teacherNotes.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
teacherNotes.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

teacherNotes.note:
Device safety: an adult must sit with the child for every AI tutor task in this unit. No child works alone with the device, and no recording is shared outside the class group.
Access: allow oral rehearsal before writing, enlarged print, sentence frames, scribing, extra time and a recorded presentation instead of a live one.
Inclusion: some families cannot supply craft materials, so keep spare card, pencils and glue in class. Assess the English evidence, never the artwork or the materials.
If a learner is anxious about presenting, let them present to two friends first, or play their recording while they stand beside the table.
teacherNotes.origin: Ehel Grade 2 capstone (authored from Units 1 to 9)
teacherNotes.sourceFile: Ehel Grade 2 Year 2 rebuilt Units 1 to 9

rubrics.level1: Too quiet or too fast for listeners to follow.
rubrics.level2: Clear in places, but the pace changes and words run together.
rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and steady; a listener catches nearly every word.
rubrics.level4: Clear, steady and confident from the first sentence to the last.

rubrics.level1: Uses very few words from the review dictionary.
rubrics.level2: Uses some review words, sometimes with the wrong meaning.
rubrics.level3: Uses review words from several units accurately.
rubrics.level4: Uses review words from at least four units, each in the right meaning.

rubrics.level1: Sentences are unfinished or the verb rarely matches the subject.
rubrics.level2: Simple sentences are mostly complete, with some verb errors.
rubrics.level3: Sentences are complete and the verbs usually match the subject.
rubrics.level4: Sentences are complete and accurate, including past, present and future forms.

rubrics.level1: Does not look up and does not answer the question asked.
rubrics.level2: Looks up once or twice and gives a one-word answer.
rubrics.level3: Looks at the listener and answers the question in a full sentence.
rubrics.level4: Looks at the listener, points at the page and answers in two linked sentences.

rubrics.level1: Pages have very little on them, or the ideas do not match the topic.
rubrics.level2: Each page has one idea, but some pages are thin.
rubrics.level3: Each page has two clear ideas that match its unit topic.
rubrics.level4: Each page has two clear ideas plus one extra detail that adds real information.

rubrics.level1: Pages are in no useful order and have no labels.
rubrics.level2: Some order is visible, and a few pages carry a label.
rubrics.level3: Pages run in a sensible order and each carries a label.
rubrics.level4: Pages run from a clear beginning to a clear ending, with labels and a cover.

rubrics.level1: Uses the same few words on every page.
rubrics.level2: Uses some review words, mostly on one or two pages.
rubrics.level3: Uses review words from several units and some describing words.
rubrics.level4: Chooses precise review words and describing words that fit each page exactly.

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops are mostly missing.
rubrics.level2: Some sentences carry a capital letter and a full stop.
rubrics.level3: Most sentences are punctuated correctly and review words are spelled correctly.
rubrics.level4: Every sentence is punctuated correctly and all thirty review words are spelled correctly.

rubrics.level1: Fewer than three pages are finished and no portfolio pieces are chosen.
rubrics.level2: Three or four pages are finished and one portfolio piece is chosen.
rubrics.level3: All six pages are finished and three portfolio pieces are chosen.
rubrics.level4: All six pages, three portfolio pieces, the recording and the reflection are complete.

rubrics.level1: The pages come from only one unit.
rubrics.level2: The pages come from two or three units.
rubrics.level3: The pages come from at least four different units.
rubrics.level4: The pages come from at least four units and the child can say what each unit taught.

rubrics.level1: Does not present, or reads the page silently.
rubrics.level2: Presents briefly with a lot of prompting.
rubrics.level3: Presents the whole booklet with little prompting.
rubrics.level4: Presents the whole booklet independently and holds the listener's attention.

rubrics.level1: Makes no changes and writes no reflection.
rubrics.level2: Makes one change and writes one reflection sentence.
rubrics.level3: Makes two changes because of feedback and writes a reflection with a goal.
rubrics.level4: Makes two or more changes, explains each one, and sets a specific goal for Year 3.
